title: Integrality of the Betti Moduli Space
speaker: Johan De Jong
abstract: Let X be a smooth projective variety over the complex numbers. Let M be the moduli space of irreducible representations of the topological fundamental group of X of a fixed rank r. Then M is a finite type scheme over the spectrum of the integers Z. One may ask if the irreducible components of M surject onto Spec(Z) or whether M is pure over Z in some sense. We give a weak answer to these questions and we discuss what other phenomena can be studied using the method of proof.

proof_stages.label: Universal purity and integrality criterion
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces Raynaud--Gruson universal purity for affine finite-type Z-schemes, reformulates it as a non-infinite-divisibility condition after number-field base change, and records that dominant components then have integral points.

proof_stages.label: Betti moduli purity question and evidence
proof_stages.summary: For a smooth projective complex variety X, the representation variety Hom(pi_1(X),GL_r) and its GIT quotient are viewed as finite-type schemes over Z, and the central question is whether these Betti moduli spaces satisfy universal purity, with evidence from Simpson's integrality conjecture for rigid points and from dimension/nonemptiness results in the joint paper.

proof_stages.label: Specialization and companion strategy
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy is sketched: specialize X to a suitable finite field, transport finite-image representation data through prime-to-p fundamental groups, use arithmeticity and l-adic companions to change primes, and lift back to obtain points in desired closed fibers, though without full control of component dimensions.

proof_stages.label: Mochizuki semisimplicity reduced to a bad locus
proof_stages.summary: To illustrate the method, the speaker rederives Mochizuki's theorem that pullback of a semisimple complex local system along a morphism of normal varieties remains semisimple, reducing to smooth varieties and encoding possible counterexamples as a constructible bad locus W in moduli of irreducible representations with invariant subspaces but no splitting.

proof_stages.label: Good stratum, closed point, and finite-field deformation space
proof_stages.summary: Assuming W_Q is nonempty, one chooses a maximal smooth locally closed stratum W' and a finite-field closed point z, spreads out the morphism, specializes to a large-characteristic finite field, factors the representation through prime-to-p specialization, and identifies the completed local moduli space with a formal deformation space.

proof_stages.label: Galois action preserves the bad formal stratum
proof_stages.summary: After enlarging the finite field of definition, the arithmetic fundamental group gives an outer Galois action on the deformation space, and the key lemma is that the formal completion of W' at z is stable under this action, checked on dense Witt-vector points because nonsplitting is invariant under conjugation.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic-zero fixed point and purity contradiction
proof_stages.summary: A fixed-point theorem for the Galois action on the formally smooth deformation space produces a characteristic-zero arithmetic l-adic representation still lying in the bad locus, but irreducibility and purity of arithmetic local systems force its pullback to be semisimple, contradicting nonsplitting and proving the theorem.

examples.name: Audience test case M = Spec F_p
examples.note: The question whether Spec F_p is universally pure exposes the missing requirement that the relevant component must dominate Spec Z, so it serves to correct the formulation of the purity criterion.
examples.engagement_reason: the audience tests the definition on M = Spec F_p and the speaker checks it against the purity condition, correcting the statement to require components dominating Spec Z

examples.name: Integral representations of a smooth projective fundamental group
examples.note: The speaker mentions integral representations of the fundamental group of a smooth projective variety as an application one would get from universal purity of the corresponding moduli space.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker develops the application by constructing the Hom/GIT moduli of representations of a smooth projective fundamental group and reasoning how universal purity would yield integral representation points

examples.name: Triangle of rational curves
examples.note: A triangle made of P^1s inside a smooth projective variety is raised as a nearby restriction problem for semisimple local systems, illustrating a question not settled by the theorem being reproved.
examples.engagement_reason: the triangle of rational curves is raised only as an interesting open question about restricting local systems, with no construction or calculation carried out on it
